title_la: Central Valley anadromous salmonid habitat suitability criteria
cover_raw_la: defined cover coding system (cobble, boulder, fine woody vegetation, fine woody vegetation + overhead, branches, branches + overhead, log, log + overhead, overhead cover, undercut bank, aquatic vegetation, aquatic vegetation + overhead, rip-rap)
cover_quantified_raw_la: "The first step in the development of the cover criteria was to group cover codes, so that there were no significant differences within the groups and a significant difference between the groups, using Pearsons test for association. Categorical data (substrate and cover) are developed into HSC by calculating the frequencies for each substrate or cover code, and then dividing the frequencies by the highest frequency cover or substrate code, so that the highest HSI value is 1.0. For the Sacramento River, effects of availability on cover use were addressed by subsampling equal areas with and without woody cover... This technique could not be used for the Yuba River and Clear Creek, due to the limited availability of woody cover. As a result, the HSI for each cover group was calculated by dividing the percent of occupied locations in each group by the percent of occupied locations in the group with the highest percent of occupied locations."
habitat_use_raw_la: spawning - wading and getting direct measurements at redds (shallow), video (deep) rearing - snorkel surveys/SCUBA, depending on water depth (dropping numbered tags at fish locations, recording fish size/species/cover code, then measuring depth, velocity, and adjacent velocity at each tag; presence/absence includes sampling unoccupied locations)
cover_influence_raw_la: juveniles showed preferences for branches, branches + overhead, logs, logs + overhead, undercut banks; natural complexity was important; in Clear Creek, cobble was also preferred/optimal
drivers_raw_la: "for anadromous salmonids, preferred habitat conditions are assumed to translate into increased growth and survival" (stated but untested assumption); paper suggests that cover allows for food availability via drift, using cover-provided velocity refuges; importantly, this paper evaluates habitat selection but not habitat's influence on fish outcomes (growth, survival, etc.)
include_raw_la: yes - directly applicable, full treatment and discussion of cover related to observed Central Valley salmonid habitat preferences across multiple tributaries/rivers
